brief_document.title: CLI error message improvements for operator clarity
brief_document.purpose: Several CLI and API commands return vague or misleading errors that force operators to guess what went wrong. A bad workflow type returns generic 'not found' instead of naming the unregistered type. A nonexistent workflow ID returns 'invalid argument' instead of 'workflow not found'. Signalling a completed workflow returns accepted:true instead of an error. Cancelling a completed workflow says 'process 0 is not live' instead of 'workflow has already completed'. These messages waste operator time and make the system harder to adopt.
brief_document.task: Improve error messages across the CLI commands and server API handlers so that each failure mode returns a specific, actionable message naming the entity involved and the reason for failure. Ensure terminal-state operations (signal, cancel on completed/failed/cancelled workflows) return clear errors rather than misleading success or cryptic internal messages.

requirements.title: Workflow type not found returns specific error
requirements.spec: WHEN a user starts a workflow with a type that is not registered, THE SYSTEM SHALL return an error message of the form 'workflow type <name> is not registered' rather than a generic 'not found'.
acceptance: CLI start with a nonexistent workflow type returns 'workflow type <name> is not registered'.
The error includes the exact type string the user provided.
The HTTP/gRPC response uses an appropriate status code (404 or INVALID_ARGUMENT).

requirements.title: Workflow not found returns specific error
requirements.spec: WHEN a user describes, signals, queries, or cancels a workflow ID that does not exist, THE SYSTEM SHALL return 'workflow <id> not found' rather than 'invalid argument'.
acceptance: CLI describe with a nonexistent workflow ID returns 'workflow <id> not found'.
CLI signal with a nonexistent workflow ID returns 'workflow <id> not found'.
CLI cancel with a nonexistent workflow ID returns 'workflow <id> not found'.
The error includes the workflow ID the user provided.

requirements.title: Signal on terminal workflow returns error
requirements.spec: WHEN a user sends a signal to a workflow in a terminal state (Completed, Failed, Cancelled, TimedOut), THE SYSTEM SHALL return an error indicating the workflow has already reached terminal state rather than returning accepted:true.
acceptance: CLI signal on a completed workflow returns an error mentioning the terminal status.
CLI signal on a failed workflow returns an error mentioning the terminal status.
The response does not return accepted:true for terminal workflows.
The error message includes the workflow ID and its current status.

requirements.title: Cancel on terminal workflow returns clear error
requirements.spec: WHEN a user cancels a workflow that is already in a terminal state, THE SYSTEM SHALL return 'workflow <id> has already completed with status <status>' rather than 'process 0 is not live'.
acceptance: CLI cancel on a completed workflow returns 'workflow <id> has already completed with status Completed'.
CLI cancel on a failed workflow returns a message including 'Failed' status.
No reference to 'process 0 is not live' appears in user-facing output.
The error message includes the workflow ID.

boundaries: SHALL NOT change the CLI argument parsing or command structure.
SHALL NOT change the gRPC or HTTP protocol message schemas.
SHALL only modify error message content and status code mapping, not control flow.
SHALL NOT change the engine's internal error types — only the API-layer mapping to user-facing messages.
verification: Manual test: start with bad workflow type shows specific type name in error.
Manual test: describe nonexistent workflow ID shows 'workflow <id> not found'.
Manual test: signal a completed workflow returns error with terminal status.
Manual test: cancel a completed workflow returns error with status, not 'process 0 is not live'.
